title: 私募股权支持下的顶级 CEO 有何不同之处
author: Samantha Allison, Taavo Godtfredsen, Nada Hashmi
content:
Private equity

by Samantha Allison, Taavo Godtfredsen and Nada Hashmi

April 9, 2026
HBR Staff/WhiteTea/Getty Images
  • Post

Summary.   

A two-year study of CEOs at private equity-backed companies that consistently deliver outsized returns found that they excel by mastering five behaviors: 1) They create strategic clarity; 2) They align talent to growth ambitions; 3) They maintain
Leer en españolLer em português
  • Post

Private equity-backed companies operate against an unforgiving clock. CEOs are expected to create significant value in compressed timeframes, meet aggressive growth targets, and navigate a minefield of internal and external pressures. Yet despite exhaustive vetting, significant financial incentives, and deep leadership experience, more than 50% of CEOs fail to meet expectations and are replaced during the investment period. 

That disconnect led us to ask a simple question: What truly differentiates the CEOs who consistently deliver exceptional returns from those who fall short? That inquiry became the foundation of our research and ultimately our book, The 5x CEO.

To move beyond anecdote and isolate the patterns behind sustained outperformance, we conducted a two-year research study involving more than 75 in-depth interviews with private equity-backed CEOs and investors, representing more than 900 years of combined private equity experience.  

We focused specifically on a “super-performer” cohort: 53 CEOs who led businesses that generated, on average, a 6.2x multiple on invested capital (MOIC)—more than double the typical industry target. We found that these leaders didn’t rely on a single standout strength. Instead, they demonstrated mastery across a distinct set of five leadership disciplines—actions they consistently took to shape direction, align people, and drive execution.

It’s no surprise that these disciplines matter. What differentiates the best CEOs in our sample is the degree of mastery and the consistency with which they operationalize them. Our findings suggest that in the PE world, performance is a deliberate architecture of behavior and technique. Five behaviors in particular stood out.

1. They create strategic clarity to align the organization.

The top-performing CEOs in our study don’t allow the strategy to be trapped at the top of the organization. They excel at translating an investment thesis into a three-to-five-year strategic plan. They clearly define and communicate their organization’s most important objectives, priorities, and value creation drivers, and then ensure alignment and focus across the organization. In these organizations, everyone—from the boardroom to the front line—understands how their work connects to value creation.

To make strategy visible and sticky, the CEOs we studied simplified complex goals into digestible messages, repeating them in every forum, and embedding them into the operating rhythm. They understand strategic clarity is not a quarterly conversation, it’s a drumbeat. They also recognize that clarity offers a shared language across functions and geographies, minimizing confusion and misalignment. Clarity becomes not only an alignment tool but a guardrail for focus and speed. However, they do not confuse clarity with certainty; they’re transparent about what’s fixed and what may change, creating confidence even amid ambiguity.

In a private equity-backed analytics company we studied, the CEO translated the investment thesis into a single-page, three-year plan within his first three months in the role. The plan clarified why the business existed, what winning looked like, and the few strategic priorities that mattered most—each tied to specific goals, metrics, and initiatives.

What stood out was not the framework itself, but how deeply it penetrated the organization. As the CEO shared with us, “Everybody from the leadership team to the receptionist understands where they fit within the plan, how they’re accountable to it, and how they’re helping to build a sustainable company.”

To test strategic clarity in your own organization, ask yourself:

  • Have we clearly defined our top three strategic priorities for the next three years?
  • Can every executive on the team articulate those priorities the same way?
  • Are we aligning board discussions, team meetings, initiatives, and individual goals around the same strategic spine?

2. They ensure they have the talent to match their growth ambitions.

CEOs who deliver outsized returns don’t just hire great people, they ensure that their most critical roles are filled by top talent who can scale with the business. This means they make tough calls early, build leadership bench strength, and structure teams for tomorrow’s complexity, not today’s comfort.

These leaders take a disciplined, data-informed approach to talent. They rigorously assess current leaders and make difficult talent decisions quickly to avoid organizational drag. And they don’t just fill gaps; they raise the bar by bringing in proven scale leaders who “know what good looks like” and can hit the ground running. Talent strategy is embedded into the value-creation plan, not an afterthought.

They’re also intentional about designing the organization two steps ahead. They create roles that anticipate growth rather than react to it, and they ensure that succession planning, leadership development, and cultural fit are woven into hiring and performance management processes. These CEOs are constantly asking: Is this team built for the company we want to become? 

A healthcare CEO in our research conducted quarterly talent reviews with the board in which they assessed leaders in critical roles across performance, potential, flight risk, and succession readiness. They discussed talent-related risks that could delay execution or impair returns with the same rigor as financial risk, making people decisions a standing governance mechanism rather than an episodic discussion.

To test whether you have the talent to match your ambitions, ask yourself:

  • Do we have A-player talent in the seats that matter most for the next stage of growth?
  • Are we objectively assessing our leaders against what the business needs and moving fast enough to upgrade where we are short?
  • What key talent changes need to happen in the next 90 days to accelerate value?

3. They relentlessly focus on high-impact priorities.

Amidst the chaos of growth and change, high-performing CEOs stay laser-focused. They resist the temptation to chase too many initiatives, instead ensuring that teams are executing against a few “big rocks” that truly move the needle. They protect time, energy, and capital for what matters most.

In our research, we repeatedly saw CEOs who had the courage to say no. They understood that strategy is as much about what you choose not to do as what you pursue. With their focus on strategic clarity, they limited active initiatives to three to five major priorities and made sure those were crystal clear throughout the organization. They created visual tools like dashboards and stop/start/continue lists to reinforce focus and communicate tradeoffs.

This discipline was reinforced through how they resourced their time. These CEOs restructured meeting cadences to center around strategic priorities. Leadership team meetings weren’t status updates; they were decision-making forums tied directly to progress against the value-creation plan. They measured not only lagging indicators like revenue and EBITDA, but also leading indicators tied to focus areas such as pipeline growth, new business wins, or schedule capacity utilization. At their core, 5x CEOs are not just focused; they are relentlessly intolerant of drift, ambiguity, and missed commitments.

“Every company I have looked at that has not been doing well has been because of a failure to focus,” one of the most successful CEOs in our study who ran several PE-backed retail companies shared with us. For her, focus was not a matter of intent but of discipline. She reinforced it through a simple priority-setting mechanism with her leadership team, regularly bucketing initiatives into what matters most now, what comes next, and what needs to come off the list altogether or realistically wait until the next hold period. The exercise forced explicit tradeoffs, prevented new opportunities from diluting execution, and provided a clear way to stay aligned with the board as priorities evolved.

To examine your own strategic discipline, ask yourself:

  • Are we spending time on what matters or what’s urgent?
  • What projects or meetings could we stop doing tomorrow with minimal negative impact?
  • Do we have a cadence for re-evaluating and realigning focus across the company?

4. They create agile operating rhythms to ensure disciplined execution.

The most successful CEOs in our study turn strategy into action by creating the rhythm, dashboards, and decision forums that drive accountability. These systems build momentum while remaining agile enough to adjust course based on results and real-time feedback.

These leaders implement weekly, monthly, and quarterly routines with intent. They don’t default to inherited operating models. They design new ones that match their company’s pace and goals, connecting long-term strategy with short-term action. They ensure that progress against the value-creation plan is tracked and discussed regularly, with the same rigor as financials. When execution falters, it’s caught early and corrected quickly, not months later at a board meeting.

Importantly, the operating rhythm is not just for senior leaders. CEOs push accountability down into the organization, enabling frontline teams to take ownership and flag issues early. They equip teams with shared KPIs, rapid feedback loops, and clear escalation paths. This allows the organization to operate at a faster pace without burning out.

Another CEO in our study from the healthcare services industry believes that disciplined execution starts by helping employees understand “the method to the madness—how values, strategy, talent, and operations all connect.” To reinforce this, he created a visual that showed how the company’s key meetings, reviews, and planning processes fit together over the year.

The year began with a kickoff to set priorities and the annual operating plan. That plan fed into talent reviews and an employee survey, followed by a spring refresh of the strategy. The updated strategy was then reviewed with the board and used to inform the next year’s operating plan. Throughout the year, a regular cadence of daily, weekly, and monthly reviews tied day-to-day execution back to these broader priorities.

By making the system visible, the CEO helped the organization see how execution actually worked—and reinforced alignment through structure, not reminders.

To assess your operating rhythms, ask yourself:

  • Are we clear on who owns what and by when across our top initiatives?
  • Do we have a reliable cadence for reviewing performance and making course corrections?
  • How fast can we identify issues, escalate decisions, and reallocate resources?

5. They build cultures that balance trust and accountability.

The high-performing CEOs in our study understand that culture is what they tolerate. They don’t rely on perks or slogans to drive it, and they’re intentional about the tone they set, the values and behaviors they reward, and the norms they allow to persist. They model urgency, transparency, and high standards.

Many build “ownership cultures” where employees feel responsible for results and are empowered to act. This requires pairing high expectations with psychological safety and trust, ensuring employees can feel comfortable raising issues, experimenting, and owning mistakes.

For example, an industrial services CEO in our study translated their company values—accountability, collaboration, and empowerment—directly into job descriptions. Known as ACE documents, these descriptions clearly specify what each role owns, how it is expected to collaborate, where decision authority sits, and how success is measured. The ACE document removes ambiguity and makes the company’s values tangible in day-to-day work across the organization.

See more HBR charts in Data & Visuals

To assess your culture, ask yourself:

  • Are we building a culture that people want to be part of or just endure?
  • How do we respond when people make mistakes or raise hard truths?
  • Do our leaders consistently model the behavior we expect from others?

. . .

What ultimately differentiates 5x CEOs is their ability to design leadership disciplines that scale beyond their direct involvement. They build operating systems that generate alignment, focus, and momentum across the organization. In the highest-performing companies we studied, these disciplines functioned as accountability mechanisms, making underperformance difficult to ignore or rationalize. Our data suggests this is less a matter of leadership style than a system of enforcement.

The implication is straightforward. Sustainable outperformance comes from deliberately designing a small number of reinforcing systems: establishing strategic clarity, aligning and scaling talent, maintaining relentless focus, enforcing disciplined execution, and building cultures of accountability and trust. Top performing PE-backed CEOs approach their role as architects—embedding these disciplines into how the company actually operates—ensuring sustained performance and delivering for all stakeholders.

Samantha Allison is the coauthor of The 5x CEO, cofounder of AdvantageCEO, and a board advisor and executive coach to private equity–backed CEOs and leadership teams. She works with executives to accelerate performance, scale leadership, and unlock enterprise value, drawing on more than 25 years of leadership experience, including roles as a senior executive at GE.
Taavo Godtfredsen is the coauthor of The 5x CEO and cofounder of AdvantageCEO, where he advises private equity investors and portfolio company CEOs. A former business owner with a successful exit, he is a member of the Marshall Goldsmith 100 Coaches organization and holds multiple coaching certifications, including in executive and team coaching.
Nada Hashmi is an assistant professor of information technology at Babson College. Her research examines how technology—including AI and digital collaboration tools—shapes leadership, collective intelligence, and team performance in organizations.

aiSummary: 一项针对私募股权支持企业中高绩效 CEO 的两年期研究表明，持续的卓越表现源于对五项领导力准则的掌握：战略清晰度、人才匹配、聚焦执行、敏捷运营节奏以及问责文化。
oneSentenceSummary: 一项针对私募股权支持企业中高绩效 CEO 的两年期研究表明，持续的卓越表现源于对五项领导力准则的掌握：战略清晰度、人才匹配、聚焦执行、敏捷运营节奏以及问责文化。
summary: 本文探讨了私募股权支持企业中“超级表现者”CEO 的行为模式。研究基于对 53 位领导者的两年跟踪调查，这些领导者实现了平均 6.2 倍的投资资本回报。作者认为，这些领导者并非依赖单一优势，而是构建了一套由五项准则相互强化的系统：通过建立战略清晰度来统一组织目标，确保人才与增长雄心相匹配，保持对高影响力优先事项的持续关注，建立敏捷的运营节奏以推动执行，并营造一种平衡信任与问责的文化。研究强调，在私募股权的高压环境下，成功与其说是个人领导风格的体现，不如说是对这些运营系统刻意且持续的执行。

mainPoints.point: 掌握五项特定的领导力准则可推动持续的卓越表现。
mainPoints.explanation: 表现顶尖的 CEO 将领导力视为一项架构任务，持续强化战略清晰度、人才匹配、聚焦、敏捷节奏和问责制，以确保组织有效规模化。

mainPoints.point: 战略清晰度必须是“持续的鼓点”，而非季度性事件。
mainPoints.explanation: 成功的领导者将复杂的投资论点转化为简单、可重复的信息，使其渗透到组织的各个层面，确保每位员工都理解自己在价值创造中的角色。

mainPoints.point: 人才战略必须融入价值创造计划中。
mainPoints.explanation: 高绩效 CEO 像对待财务风险一样严谨地对待人事决策，主动升级人才以应对未来的复杂性，而不仅仅是填补当前的空缺。

mainPoints.point: 执行力需要敏捷的运营节奏和不懈的专注。
mainPoints.explanation: 通过将倡议限制在少数几个“核心重点”上，并设计优先考虑决策而非状态更新的会议节奏，领导者可以防止组织偏离目标并确保问责制。

keyQuotes: 我们的研究结果表明，在私募股权领域，绩效是行为和技巧的刻意架构。
他们明白战略清晰度不是季度性对话，而是持续的鼓点。
5 倍回报的 CEO 最终的区别在于他们设计领导力准则的能力，这些准则能够超越他们个人的直接参与而发挥作用。
我们研究中的高绩效 CEO 明白，文化就是你所容忍的一切。
